name: San Diego Unified School District
districtFact: Founded in 1854 — among the oldest school districts in California — San Diego Unified is the second-largest district in the state and serves one of the most geographically distinctive student populations in the country: a city defined by the largest concentration of active-duty military personnel in the United States and a cross-border community with deep ties to Tijuana and Baja California.

about.title: About San Diego Unified School District
about.content: San Diego Unified is California's second-largest school district — 100,000 students, more than 225 schools, operating since 1854. The district runs neighborhood schools alongside an extensive school choice system: magnet programs in Dual Language Immersion (Spanish and Mandarin), performing arts, STEM, and IB at select middle and high campuses. The part that consistently surprises new families: neighborhood school assignment and magnet enrollment are entirely separate processes. Your assigned school comes from your address and is automatic. A spot in Dual Language Immersion, IB, or a STEM magnet requires a separate application — and popular programs run lotteries when demand exceeds seats. Many families who miss the initial application window end up on waitlists or waiting until the next enrollment cycle.

about.title: School Choice and Magnet Programs
about.content: Magnet and choice programs are among the most searched topics in SDUSD parent communities — for good reason. Dual Language Immersion in particular receives far more applications than available seats at most campuses; lottery outcomes, not just interest, determine who gets in. Your neighborhood school is guaranteed by your address. A magnet school is not. Parents moving to San Diego specifically for a program should confirm with the school choice office that the program is actively enrolling before committing to a lease — application windows and seat counts shift year to year, and waitlists can be long.

planningTips: If you're moving mid-year and hoping to enroll in a magnet or Dual Language program, most application windows open December–February for the following fall. Arriving in March or April, you've probably missed the window for choice programs that year. Your neighborhood school will enroll you — contact the school choice office anyway to get on waitlists.

SDUSD's Spring Recess (late March) and Mexican public school spring break (typically mid-April) fall about two weeks apart and don't overlap. Cross-border families manage two separate school calendars — check both each year.
San Diego's mild weather makes late-July back-to-school prep comfortable, but uniform and supply retailers near school zones sell out of common sizes fast. Shop before July 28.

Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) is a California state holiday — schools are closed. In years when it falls outside Spring Recess, it's a day off that won't appear on any non-California school calendar. Families arriving from other states are frequently surprised by it.

districtFaqs.q: Does San Diego Unified have support for military families?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. With Camp Pendleton, NAS North Island, MCAS Miramar, and Naval Base San Diego all nearby, SDUSD handles mid-year military transfers routinely. The district's Military Family Liaison coordinates expedited enrollment — contact them before your PCS move date, not after. Unofficial records are typically accepted while transcripts are in transit.

districtFaqs.q: How does SDUSD's calendar affect families with ties to Tijuana or Baja California?
districtFaqs.a: Mexican public schools follow a September-to-June calendar with spring break typically in mid-April — about two weeks after SDUSD's Spring Recess (late March). The two systems don't share a spring break window, so cross-border families need to track both calendars separately.

districtFaqs.q: Is San Diego Unified the same as San Diego State University (SDSU)?
districtFaqs.a: No. SDUSD is the K-12 public school system; SDSU is a separate public university with its own semester calendar. Neither affects the other's schedule.

districtFaqs.q: Why are school weather closures so rare in San Diego Unified?
districtFaqs.a: San Diego's Mediterranean climate means the events that cancel school elsewhere — snow, ice storms, hurricanes — almost never happen here. In most years, SDUSD finishes the year exactly as scheduled with no weather-related closures. If you're arriving from the East Coast or Midwest expecting occasional snow days, that concept essentially doesn't exist here.

districtFaqs.q: Do charter schools in San Diego follow the SDUSD calendar?
districtFaqs.a: Not necessarily. Independent charters set their own calendars — some align with SDUSD, many don't, especially those in national networks. Confirm directly with your charter school. Don't assume.

districtFaqs.q: Does SDUSD offer Dual Language or bilingual programs?
districtFaqs.a: Yes — Spanish and Mandarin Dual Language Immersion programs run at select elementary and middle school campuses. Enrollment is through a separate application process, not automatic by address. Demand at popular campuses typically exceeds available seats, making lottery outcomes the deciding factor. Contact the school choice office for the current program list and application deadlines.

districtFaqs.q: What is a Minimum Day in San Diego Unified?
districtFaqs.a: A Minimum Day is a shortened school day — students dismiss 2–3 hours earlier than usual. SDUSD schedules them throughout the year for professional development, parent-teacher conferences, and state assessments. Because they can fall on any weekday without an obvious calendar marker, they're the most common scheduling surprise for families new to the district. Minimum Day dates are published on the SDUSD calendar at the start of each school year.

districtFaqs.q: How does wildfire smoke affect SDUSD school schedules?
districtFaqs.a: Santa Ana winds from late September through November can push smoke from inland fires toward coastal San Diego. On high-AQI days, SDUSD cancels or modifies outdoor activities — recess, PE — though full school closures for air quality are rare. Check the San Diego Air Quality Management District during fall fire season.

livingHere.intro: San Diego has more active-duty military personnel than any other US city, sits 15 miles from Tijuana, and has a school system that's been running since before California became a state. Those aren't just background facts — they shape what enrollment actually looks like, which programs fill up fast, and which calendar details matter most for daily life.

highlights.label: Military Families and Mid-Year Enrollment
highlights.detail: Camp Pendleton, Naval Air Station North Island, MCAS Miramar, and Naval Base San Diego make this the most military-dense city in the country. PCS transfers arrive at SDUSD throughout the year — not just in August. The district's Military Family Liaison handles expedited mid-year enrollment, and the process moves faster when families contact the office before the move. The timing mismatch is real: the magnet application window (typically December–February) doesn't align with most PCS cycles. Arriving in spring, the neighborhood school enrollment moves fast — the choice program window for that year is usually already closed.

highlights.label: The US–Mexico Border and Cross-Border Families
highlights.detail: The San Ysidro Port of Entry is 15 miles from downtown and one of the world's busiest land crossings. Morning northbound wait times regularly run 60–90 minutes — longer on Mondays and Fridays. For families who cross daily for school, that wait is part of the commute. The calendar gap adds another layer: SDUSD Spring Recess falls in late March, while Mexican public school spring break typically runs mid-April. Two systems, two different weeks off. Cross-border families track both every year, and the breaks don't line up.

highlights.label: Biotech, Defense, and Research Employment
highlights.detail: The families who arrive mid-year from biotech and defense relocations tend to know exactly what programs they want before the moving truck shows up. Qualcomm, Illumina, and a dense cluster of life sciences companies and defense contractors draw employees from competitive school districts across the country — people who've been tracking STEM programs and IB access for years before the offer letter came. UC San Diego, Scripps Research, and the Salk Institute add a research university layer on top of the corporate one. STEM magnets and IB pathways at SDUSD see consistent demand from this group, and application windows don't adjust for corporate relocation schedules.

highlights.label: Wildfire Smoke and Fall Air Quality
highlights.detail: Late September through November is Santa Ana season. When offshore winds push smoke from inland fires toward the coast, air quality in San Diego neighborhoods can spike without much warning. SDUSD cancels outdoor recess and PE on high-AQI days; full school closures for air quality are uncommon but not unheard of during active nearby fire events. Families arriving from the Pacific Northwest often expect fall rain to clear the air — in San Diego, fall is dry and warm, fire risk runs alongside the school year, and conditions can change quickly.

highlights.label: San Diego International Airport (SAN)
highlights.detail: If you're flying over Spring Recess, book early. SAN is a small airport — 3 miles from downtown, which is convenient most of the year — but the Friday SDUSD lets out for Spring Recess and the Sunday return are typically among the busiest travel periods of the year. The late-May school end creates a second busy window, particularly for families heading to Mexico or the Midwest for the summer. Families planning trips to East Asia or Europe tend to drive up to LAX, about 2 hours north, where nonstop options are substantially better and prices don't spike the same way during the school calendar breaks.

relatedDistricts.name: Los Angeles Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: The largest school district in California and the second largest in the United States. Families relocating along the Southern California coast between Los Angeles and San Diego frequently compare both districts' calendars and school programs.

relatedDistricts.name: Long Beach Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: A large Southern California district between Los Angeles and San Diego, and a common reference point for families comparing California coastal districts. Frequently compared by families in transit between San Diego and the LA metro area.
